name: Japanese Cosmology
shortDescription: Japanese cosmology describes the creation and structure of the universe according to Shinto and early Japanese beliefs. It outlines the emergence of the world from chaos and the subsequent creation of deities and the Japanese islands.
description: Japanese cosmology presents a hierarchical view of the universe, originating from a state of formless chaos. From this chaos, known as 'Khaos' or 'Ame-tsuchi hajimete hirakeshi toki' (When heaven and earth first parted), emerged the first deities, the Kotoamatsukami, who were self-created and lived in Takamagahara, the High Plain of Heaven. This heavenly realm is considered the dwelling place of the celestial gods. Below Takamagahara was the world of humans, often referred to as Ashihara no Nakatsukuni (Central Land of Reed Plains). Beneath this world was Yomi, the land of the dead, a dark and impure realm. The creation myth continues with Izanagi and Izanami, brother and sister deities, who were tasked with solidifying and creating the islands of Japan. They used the Amenonuhoko spear to stir the primordial ocean, and from the droplets that dripped off the spear, the first island, Onogoro-shima, was formed. They descended to this island and built a palace, marking the beginning of the creation of the Japanese archipelago and the birth of numerous other deities. This cosmological framework is central to Shinto beliefs, providing a foundation for understanding the relationship between gods, humans, and the natural world. The emphasis on purity and impurity, as exemplified by Izanagi's journey to Yomi and subsequent purification rituals, remains a significant aspect of Shinto practice. The Land of Reed Plains is also a contested space, later requiring the intervention of Amaterasu to bring order and peace.

sources.title: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.author: W.G. Aston
